macro_segments.title: The Mishnah on houses: beitecha versus bayit zeh and the scope of neder when ownership changes

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the Mishnah: cases of beitecha and bayit zeh with their different halachic outcomes
The Ran's distinction between beitecha and bayit zeh regarding stricter laws (chumrot) in different contexts
Tangential discussion on shivrei keilim (broken vessels) and tumah yeshanah in the context of continuity of objects
Case of sukkah in Sukkot: Rabbi Eliezer's position on sukkah falling and being rebuilt, and Tosfos's solution using same materials

macro_segments.title: Gemara question on Avimi: can a neder forbid something even after it leaves one's possession (adam oser davar shebirshuto lichshe-yeitzei me-reshuto)

micro_segments.title: Avimi's question: the principle of ein adam oser davar she'eino shelo and its application to bayit zeh
The fundamental question: can a person forbid an object that is currently in his possession even after it leaves his possession
Rava's proof from Bava Kamma: father forbidding son from benefiting, with explicit language be-chayai u-ve-moti extending prohibition after death
The distinction between implicit and explicit language in the neder formula and how it affects whether the prohibition continues
Ran's problems with Rava's proof: question of zechia b'issurei hana'ah (ownership of items forbidden to benefit from)
Second problem: how can the son pass on an inheritance he cannot inherit (lo yerishenu but can give to grandchildren)
Rav Chaim's distinction between hekdesh (which creates absolute status in the object) and nedarim (which depends on relationship to the person)

macro_segments.title: Mishna on forbidden fruits and the question of chilufeihem (proceeds from sale) versus giduleihem (produce that grows from the seed)

micro_segments.title: Mishna: forbidden fruits remain forbidden in their replacements (chilufeihem) and in their offspring (giduleihem)
Rami Bar Chama's question: do chilufeihem apply the same way when the prohibition is on someone else versus on oneself
Rami Bar Chama's analogy: comparing the power to forbid someone else's property (peirot chaveiro) with the power to forbid things that do not yet exist (davar shelo ba le'olam)
Two possibilities for resolution: either chilufeihem are like someone else's property (limited to forbidding to oneself), or they are like giduleihem (apply universally)

macro_segments.title: Rav Acha Bar Minyumi's proof from a husband forbidding his wife, and the question of whether chilufeihem count as benefit when creditors extract debt

micro_segments.title: Case: husband forbids wife from benefiting from him (konam she'ani neheneh lach), yet creditors can still extract debt
Ran's caveat: this neder must be made before nisu'in (marriage) to avoid contradicting the ketubah's obligations
The question: how can creditors extract if the husband forbade benefiting the wife; is this counted as chilufeihem
First possibility rejected: creditor extraction is not chilufeihem (not like giduleihem); it is only lekatchila forbidden but bedi'avad permitted
Rava's alternative proof: using the case of orlah (fruit from first three years) as kiddushin, which does not work, but its proceeds do work
Explanation of orlah: it is assur behana'ah, so using it as kiddushin fails; however, selling the orlah and using the money as kiddushin succeeds
Application to the wife case: just as with orlah, when there are two steps of removal (debt paid by husband), the chilufeihem are not caught in the neder
Discussion: difference between the illegal act of selling orlah and the legal status of the proceeds (money); new status attaches to the proceeds
Tangent on burning orlah: since nisrafim (things burned) attain new status through panim chadashos, ashes would not count as orlah anymore

macro_segments.title: Rambam's position on son who is forbidden from benefiting from father's inheritance, and Ra'avad's objection about passing on property one does not own

micro_segments.title: Rambam: son who is forbidden from benefiting from father can give the inheritance to his brothers or children, and it is permitted
Ra'avad's objection: how can the son pass on property he does not own; why is a condition (talmid chacham) needed if the next halacha says he can just give it away
Unresolved question: whether the son owns the property but cannot benefit from it (Ran's view), or does not own it at all (Ra'avad's implication)

macro_segments.title: Mishna on cherem formula and the distinction between things of olei bavel (communal property from returnees from Babylon) and things of the city

micro_segments.title: Mishna: cases of mutual cherem (I am cherem to you, you are cherem to me) and the distinction between olei bavel and davar shel otah ha'ir
Olei bavel property: Har HaBayit, the azarot, and wells on the road—communal property over which individuals have no exclusive rights
City property (otah ha'ir): the town square, public bathhouse, synagogue, and Torah scrolls—communally owned within a city
The radical implication: if two people make each other cherem, they cannot use shared city infrastructure like streets, rendering them housebound
Question of consistency: why is Rabbi Elazar ben Yaakov not disagreeing here if communal ownership means one person cannot forbid another's own share

macro_segments.title: Mishna on writing one's share to the Nasi and the debate between Rabbi Yehuda and the Rabbis about what constitutes valid acquisition

micro_segments.title: Solution to cherem problem: writing (ktiva) one's share in municipal property to the Nasi (governor) or a third party
Rabbi Yehuda: writing to Nasi does not require acquisition (zechiya) by the Nasi, but writing to a regular person (hedyot) does require his acquisition
Rabbis' response: both Nasi and hedyot require actual acquisition (zechiya); the mention of Nasi is just a practical example
Rabbi Yehuda: in the Galil region, people do not need to write a document because their ancestors already wrote over the land generations ago

topical_tags.term: ein adam oser davar she'eino shelo
